text: The surgeon opens the lesser sac and identifies the epiploic and middle colic veins draining into the superior mesenteric vein at the inferior pancreatic border. The mesocolon is separated from the anterior surface of the pancreatic head, and the right gastroepiploic vein is divided. The superior mesenteric vein is further exposed. The duodenum is retracted medially using a prograsper to perform a Kocher maneuver. The left renal vein is identified, and the station XIVA lymph node is removed to expose the origin of the superior mesenteric artery. In the porta hepatis, the hepatoduodenal ligament is dissected, and the right gastric vessels are divided. The station A tail lymph node is removed, exposing the common hepatic artery. The gastroduodenal artery is dissected and ligated with Hem-o-lok clips before division. The dissection of Calot's triangle is performed, and the cystic artery and cystic duct are divided.

In the intrapyloric region, the surgeon divides the right gastroepiploic vessels, preparing the duodenum approximately two centimeters distal to the pylorus for the pylorus-preserving procedure. A tunnel is dissected under the pancreatic neck, and the pancreas is divided using monopolar scissors, achieving good hemostasis with monopolar current. The pancreatic duct is identified and divided slightly away from the transaction line to facilitate future pancreatic anastomosis. The bile duct is isolated along the anterior surface of the portal vein, clamped proximally, and divided just above the cystic duct insertion. The distal end is closed, and periportal lymphadenectomy is completed.

The surgeon divides the ligament of Treitz and delivers the small bowel into the supracolic compartment. The small bowel mesentery is divided, and the pancreatic head is retracted upwards and laterally using a prograsper to initiate uncinate dissection. Smaller blood vessels are clipped, and the mesopancreas plane is entered, identifying the superior mesenteric artery. The assistant provides retraction by rolling the superior mesenteric vein and artery with a suction device. Retropancreatic lymph nodes are cleared after clipping to prevent potential leakage post-operation.

The surgeon progresses along the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), identifying and securely clipping the inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery (IPDA), which is then divided. The remaining lymphatic tissue in the region is cleared as part of a complete lymph node dissection. The superior pancreaticoduodenal vein is divided, completing the uncinate dissection. Lymph nodes near the SMA origin and behind the portal vein are removed to complete the resection. The prograsper provides retraction from the left side, aiding in the procedure. Hemostasis is confirmed, and the jejunum end is divided, with the specimen placed in a bag.

The surgeon performs a thorough wash and mobilizes the pancreas at the neck. The jejunal limb is positioned for an end-to-side pancreaticojejunostomy. IV-O or V-O PDS sutures are used to join the posterior pancreatic parenchyma to the jejunal wall, with a stay suture placed on the anterior side of the pancreatic duct for exposure. Four to five posterior sutures are placed, followed by a jejunotomy. Three posterior ductal sutures are taken, ensuring full thickness of both pancreas and jejunum. Additional sutures are placed to complete the second layer, followed by the third layer with anterior ductal sutures. The procedure concludes with anterior pancreatic capsule to jejunal seromuscular sutures, completing the fourth layer of the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a jejunotomy approximately fifteen centimeters distal to the pancreatic anastomosis to initiate the biliary anastomosis. A continuous posterior layer is created using 4-0 B-lock sutures, ensuring precise placement on both the jejunal and bile duct sides for tight approximation. A stay suture on the anterior bile duct wall enhances exposure. The posterior layer is completed at the right corner, and the anterior layer is started using interrupted 4-0 PDS sutures to prevent future stenosis or stricture formation.

The surgeon pre-places the last few sutures before knotting and packs a gauze piece around the anastomosis to check for any areas of weakness. The jejunal limb is identified from the infracolic compartment and brought up for a four-layer hand-sewn duodenal jejunostomy using vicryl or V-lock continuous sutures. A nasojejunal tube is passed into the efferent limb for potential post-operative feeding. Two soft drains are placed, one near the pancreatic jejunostomy and the other in Morrison's pouch, before extracting the specimen through a fan and steel incision.
